features.summary: JPMorgan Chase reported strong Q3 2022 results with net income of $9.7 billion and EPS of $3.12 on revenue of $33.5 billion, delivering an 18% ROTCE. The firm took discretionary investment securities losses of $959 million from repositioning the portfolio by selling U.S. Treasuries and mortgages. Net interest income excluding Markets surged 51% year-over-year to $5.7 billion driven by higher rates, while non-interest revenue declined 24% due to lower investment banking fees and securities losses. Credit remains healthy with net charge-offs of $727 million and a net reserve build of $808 million primarily in Wholesale reflecting loan growth and updated macroeconomic scenarios.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q4 2022 NII of approximately $19 billion, implying full-year 2022 NII of $66 billion, significantly above prior guidance. For 2023, they anticipate some modest decline from the Q4 run rate due to uncertainties around rates, deposit repricing, and loan growth. The firm maintains its expense outlook and expects card net charge-off rates of approximately 1.5% for full year 2022, below previous expectations. Management emphasized they don't expect to annualize the Q4 NII run rate and suggested using around $74 billion for 2023 modeling instead of the implied $76 billion annualized rate.
features.risks: Management highlighted several key uncertainties including the effects of quantitative tightening on deposits, potential deposit migration in the new technological environment, and various repricing lags across consumer and commercial segments. Jamie Dimon warned of potential economic headwinds from inflation, higher rates, oil volatility, and the war in Ukraine, describing the environment as having 'hurricane' conditions ahead. The firm noted that if unemployment rises to 5-6%, they could face $5-6 billion in additional reserves over a couple of quarters, though this would be manageable given their strong earnings power.
features.key_drivers: The primary revenue driver was the significant benefit from rising interest rates, with NII excluding Markets up 51% year-over-year despite lower deposit betas than expected. Consumer spending remains strong with combined debit and credit spend up 13% year-over-year, though cash buffers are declining as spending outpaces income growth. Investment banking revenues declined 43% due to market volatility affecting underwriting activity, while Markets revenue increased 8% driven by elevated client activity in macro products. The firm continues heavy investments in technology, talent, and branch expansion while maintaining strong credit quality across all segments.
